findings.title: Incident date in introduction conflicts with source documents
findings.description: The location is corroborated, but the stated incident date conflicts with all three source documents. The brief says March 14, 2021, while the medical records, police report, and witness statement all identify March 12, 2021.

evidence.quote: This action arises from a workplace incident on March 14, 2021, at a commercial construction site located at 2200 West Olympic Boulevard, Los Angeles, California.
Patient was brought in by LAFD paramedics following a workplace accident occurring at approximately 10:45 AM on March 12, 2021.
Date of Incident:  March 12, 2021
Location:          2200 West Olympic Boulevard, Los Angeles, CA 90006

I, Diane Tran, provide the following statement of my own free will regarding the incident that occurred on March 12, 2021, at the construction site located at 2200 West Olympic Boulevard, Los Angeles, California.

findings.confidence_reasoning: The brief’s March 14, 2021 date directly conflicts with three independent source-document quotations giving March 12, 2021. The conflict is verbatim and mechanically checkable.

findings.title: PPE allegation conflicts with police report and witness statement
findings.description: The brief says Rivera was not wearing required PPE, including fall-arrest equipment. The police report and witness statement state the opposite: he was wearing a hard hat, harness, high-visibility vest, and his lanyard detached because the anchor point collapsed.
findings.brief_location: II. STATEMENT OF UNDISPUTED MATERIAL FACTS, Fact 4

evidence.quote: Rivera was not wearing required personal protective equipment at the time of the incident, including fall-arrest equipment mandated by site safety protocols.
Site supervisor Mark Ellison confirmed that Rivera was wearing a hard hat and harness consistent with site requirements at the time of the collapse.
The harness lanyard was found still attached to Rivera but had pulled free from the scaffolding anchor point when the structure gave way.
Carlos was wearing his hard hat, safety harness, and high-visibility vest, consistent with site PPE requirements.
His harness was still on, but the lanyard had come free â€” the anchor point it was attached to was part of the scaffolding that collapsed.

findings.confidence_reasoning: The brief’s assertion that Rivera lacked PPE is directly contradicted by both the police report and witness statement, which specify the PPE he was wearing. The harness/lanyard quotations also directly rebut the fall-arrest-equipment component.

findings.title: Witness statement contradicts assertion that Rivera presented no rebuttal evidence
findings.description: The source file contains a plaintiff-side witness statement giving evidence of unsafe scaffold conditions and Harmon foreman Donner’s alleged notice and dismissal of a base-plate concern. That conflicts with the brief’s assertion that Rivera presented no rebuttal evidence on worksite safety.

evidence.quote: Rivera has presented no evidence to rebut this presumption.
I noticed several things that concerned me:
1. Some of the cross-braces on the lower tier appeared to have significant surface rust and looked like they had been reused from a previous job.

2. The base plate on the far-east end was sitting on a piece of plywood rather than on a proper mudsill or stable footing. The plywood had shifted noticeably since I had last seen it a few days earlier.
3. Two of the coupling pins on the second tier looked bent or worn.
I also mentioned the base plate issue directly to Ray Donner.
Donner told me, "We don't have time to re-do the base. It's been fine. Just get up there and get it done."

findings.confidence_reasoning: The witness statement is evidence of unsafe scaffold conditions and notice to Harmon’s foreman, so it contradicts a blanket “no evidence” assertion. Confidence is not perfect because the record excerpt does not establish whether that witness statement was procedurally submitted by Rivera in opposition.

findings.title: Sources undercut claim that only Apex was responsible for scaffolding operations and safety
findings.description: The sources support that Apex was Rivera’s employer, but they undercut the brief’s framing that Harmon was not responsible for scaffolding operations or safety. Harmon’s foreman directed Rivera’s crew to use the east-side scaffolding, and concerns about the scaffolding condition were communicated to and allegedly dismissed by him.

evidence.quote: Here, the undisputed facts establish that Apex Staffing Solutions â€” not Harmon â€” was the employer responsible for scaffolding operations and for ensuring its employees followed appropriate safety procedures.
Employer:      Apex Staffing Solutions
Donner stated that he had directed Rivera and his crew to begin work on the east-side scaffolding section earlier that morning.
Tran stated that these concerns were communicated to both Ellison and Donner.
During our morning coordination meeting, Ray Donner â€” the project foreman for Harmon Construction â€” told us that the east-side scaffolding section needed to be fully operational by end of day.
Donner told me, "We don't have time to re-do the base. It's been fine. Just get up there and get it done."
I want to note that Donner had personally directed us to work on that section that morning and dismissed my concern about the base plate.

findings.confidence_reasoning: The record quotations show Harmon’s foreman directed work on the scaffolding and allegedly dismissed safety concerns, which materially undercuts the brief’s “Apex—not Harmon” responsibility framing. Confidence is slightly below mechanical certainty because ultimate responsibility is a legal/factual inference, not established solely by the quotations.

findings.title: Quotation attributed to Privette v. Superior Court appears materially overbroad
findings.description: Privette is a well-known California Supreme Court case, but the quoted sentence is not a faithful statement of its holding. The word “never” is materially overbroad: Privette rejected peculiar-risk liability for injuries to a contractor’s own employee, but California’s Privette line does not categorically bar every claim by a contractor’s employee against a hirer. Known actual language/rule: Privette held that the peculiar-risk doctrine does not permit an independent contractor’s employee, injured in the course of the contracted work, to recover tort damages from the person who hired the contractor. The Privette doctrine is a general rule/presumption of delegation, not an absolute “never liable” immunity; later California cases recognize exceptions such as retained control with affirmative contribution and concealed hazards.

evidence.quote: A hirer is never liable for injuries sustained by an independent contractor's employees when the injuries arise from the contracted work.

findings.confidence_reasoning: Privette’s general rule and recognized exceptions are canonical California tort doctrine, making the quoted absolute “never liable” formulation materially suspect. Confidence is not higher because the provided evidence does not include the actual Privette text or reporter quotation for a verbatim comparison.

findings.title: Cited authority could not be located: Kellerman v. Pacific Coast Construction, Inc.
findings.description: The cited case, Kellerman v. Pacific Coast Construction, Inc., 887 F.2d 1204 (9th Cir. 1991), could not be verified and has several red flags, including an unusually tailored OSHA presumption rule and an apparent date/volume mismatch. Even if real, a Ninth Circuit decision would be persuasive rather than binding in California Superior Court on California negligence law.

evidence.quote: Kellerman v. Pacific Coast Construction, Inc., 887 F.2d 1204, 1209 (9th Cir. 1991)

findings.confidence_reasoning: The evidence shows the brief cites the case, but the finding rests on inability to locate/recognize the authority and bibliographic red flags rather than a provided authoritative citation database result. It is kept because the volume/date mismatch and unusually specific purported rule are concrete warning signs.

findings.title: Filing-delay calculation conflicts with supported incident date and is arithmetically inconsistent
findings.description: The filing date itself is not supported by the provided documents, and the elapsed-time calculation conflicts with the source-supported incident date. From March 12, 2021 to March 10, 2023 is 728 days, i.e., one year and 363 days after the first year; using the brief’s own March 14 date would be 726 days, i.e., one year and 361 days, not one year and 362 days.

evidence.quote: Rivera did not file his complaint until March 10, 2023 â€” one year and 362 days after the incident.
Date of Incident:  March 12, 2021
Patient was brought in by LAFD paramedics following a workplace accident occurring at approximately 10:45 AM on March 12, 2021.

findings.confidence_reasoning: The source-supported incident date is March 12, 2021, and the day count to March 10, 2023 is mechanically calculable; the brief’s stated elapsed time does not match either the supported date or its own March 14 date. Severity is reduced because the one- or two-day arithmetic discrepancy appears less outcome-determinative than the broader limitations framing.

findings.title: Legal arguments advanced without supporting authority
findings.description: The brief states and applies rules of law without citing any case, statute, or regulation in support. The uncited assertions concern assumption of risk and a potential accrual-date challenge.
findings.brief_location: Section III.C, paragraph 2; Section III.D, paragraph 3

evidence.quote: Under the doctrine of assumption of risk, a plaintiff who voluntarily encounters a known danger cannot recover for injuries arising from that danger.

While Rivera's filing falls nominally within this window, Harmon reserves the right to challenge the accrual date based on Rivera's own knowledge of his injuries, which the medical records indicate were immediately apparent.

findings.confidence_reasoning: The quoted passages contain legal propositions but no accompanying citation in the provided brief excerpts. The finding is limited to lack of cited authority, which is directly checkable from the quoted text.

findings.title: Time-barred heading is undercut by conceded filing within limitations window
findings.description: The section heading asserts the claims are time-barred, but the body concedes the filing falls within this window under the statute of limitations. Filing on 2023-03-10 is within two years of either the brief’s March 14, 2021 date or the source-supported March 12, 2021 date, making the time-barred framing self-defeating absent a supported accrual theory.

evidence.quote: D. Rivera's Claims Are Time-Barred

While Rivera's filing falls nominally within this window, Harmon reserves the right to challenge the accrual date based on Rivera's own knowledge of his injuries, which the medical records indicate were immediately apparent.

findings.confidence_reasoning: The heading and body text are in tension because the body expressly says the filing falls within the limitations window. Confidence depends partly on the standard two-year personal-injury limitations rule and on interpreting the unsupported accrual reservation, so it is below mechanical certainty.

findings.title: Seabright is cited for an overbroad OSHA-compliance proposition
findings.description: Seabright is not principally a case about OSHA compliance being 'highly probative' of due care. It supports the Privette delegation doctrine as applied to workplace safety regulations, including Cal-OSHA, not a broad evidentiary rule that compliance with statutory safety requirements establishes or strongly proves reasonable care. The brief also overstates the point by suggesting OSHA compliance effectively insulates Harmon from tort liability; Privette exceptions may still matter.

evidence.quote: Seabright Insurance Co. v. US Airways, Inc., 52 Cal.4th 590, 598 (2011)

findings.confidence_reasoning: The legal characterization of Seabright is strongly supported by the well-known Privette/delegation doctrine, but the provided evidence includes only the citation rather than the brief’s full surrounding proposition or the case text. Because this rests partly on legal knowledge, confidence is below the mechanical-check range.

judicial_memo: The brief contains several significant factual conflicts with the provided record, along with some unsupported legal and factual assertions. Most seriously, the introduction states the incident occurred on March 14, 2021, while the medical records, police report, and witness statement all identify March 12, 2021. The statement of facts also asserts that Rivera was not wearing required PPE, including fall-arrest equipment, but the police report and witness statement say he was wearing a hard hat, harness, high-visibility vest, and that his lanyard detached when the anchor point collapsed. The brief’s assertion that Rivera presented no rebuttal evidence on worksite safety is contradicted by a plaintiff-side witness statement concerning unsafe scaffold conditions and notice to Harmon’s foreman. The sources also undercut the claim that only Apex was responsible for scaffolding operations and safety. In addition, a quotation attributed to Privette appears materially overbroad, one cited case could not be located, and several assertions lack record or legal support.

citation_verdicts.existence_reasoning: I recognize Privette v. Superior Court as a California Supreme Court decision reported at 5 Cal.4th 689 in 1993; the reporter, court, and year are consistent.
citation_verdicts.known_actual_holding: Privette held that a person who hires an independent contractor generally is not liable under the peculiar-risk doctrine for injuries to the contractor's employees, because workers' compensation is the employee's remedy against the employer and it would be anomalous to impose greater liability on the hirer. Later California cases describe this as a presumption of delegation/nonliability subject to exceptions.
citation_verdicts.support_reasoning: The asserted proposition accurately states the general Privette rule: a hirer is presumptively not liable for injuries sustained by an independent contractor's employees while performing the contracted work. The brief should still account for recognized exceptions developed in cases such as Hooker, McKown, Kinsman, and later Privette-line cases.

citation_verdicts.existence_reasoning: Same case as cite-1: Privette v. Superior Court, 5 Cal.4th 689 (1993), is a real California Supreme Court case. I do not recognize the quoted absolute sentence as an actual holding or quotation, and its wording is suspiciously overbroad.
citation_verdicts.known_actual_holding: Privette established a general rule/presumption that a hirer of an independent contractor is not liable for work-related injuries to the contractor's employees under the peculiar-risk doctrine. The doctrine is not absolute; California recognizes exceptions, including retained control with affirmative contribution, furnishing defective equipment, and concealed hazards known to the hirer.
citation_verdicts.support_reasoning: Privette supports a general presumption of hirer nonliability, but the brief overstates it by saying the hirer is 'never' liable. Controlling California authority recognizes exceptions, including Hooker v. Department of Transportation, McKown v. Wal-Mart Stores, Inc., and Kinsman v. Unocal Corp. Whether Rivera's claims are barred would require analyzing those exceptions, especially any retained control or affirmative contribution by Harmon.

citation_verdicts.existence_reasoning: I do not recognize Whitmore v. Delgado Scaffolding Co. or the citation 334 F. Supp. 2d 1189 (C.D. Cal. 2004). The reporter/year combination is plausible and I have no definitive mismatch, but the parenthetical is fact-specific and conveniently aligned with the brief's scaffolding facts, so I cannot verify it from memory.
citation_verdicts.support_reasoning: Because I cannot verify the existence or holding of the case, I cannot assess whether it supports the asserted proposition.
citation_verdicts.jurisdiction_concern: A C.D. Cal. federal district court decision applying California law would be persuasive, not binding, in California Superior Court.

citation_verdicts.existence_reasoning: I do not recognize Kellerman v. Pacific Coast Construction, Inc., 887 F.2d 1204 (9th Cir. 1991). There are concrete red flags: the quoted OSHA 'rebuttable presumption' rule sounds unusually tailored to the brief, I do not know of such a Ninth Circuit rule creating a presumption of due care from OSHA compliance, and volume 887 F.2d is more consistent with the late 1980s than a 1991 Ninth Circuit decision.
citation_verdicts.support_reasoning: Because the case appears likely fabricated or at least cannot be authenticated from memory, I cannot credit it as supporting the OSHA-compliance presumption. As a substantive matter, California law more commonly treats violation of safety statutes as potential evidence/negligence per se; compliance does not generally create an automatic presumption insulating a defendant from negligence liability.
citation_verdicts.jurisdiction_concern: Even if real, a Ninth Circuit decision would be persuasive, not binding, on a California Superior Court deciding California negligence law.

citation_verdicts.existence_reasoning: I recognize Seabright Insurance Co. v. US Airways, Inc., 52 Cal.4th 590 (2011), as a California Supreme Court Privette-line case; the citation details are consistent.
citation_verdicts.known_actual_holding: Seabright held that a hirer generally delegates to an independent contractor any tort duty the hirer owes to the contractor's employees to comply with workplace safety requirements, including Cal-OSHA regulations. The hirer's alleged statutory/regulatory safety duties therefore did not create liability to the contractor's employee in that case.
citation_verdicts.support_reasoning: Seabright is not principally a case about OSHA compliance being 'highly probative' of due care. It supports the Privette delegation doctrine as applied to workplace safety regulations, including Cal-OSHA, not a broad evidentiary rule that compliance with statutory safety requirements establishes or strongly proves reasonable care. The brief also overstates the point by suggesting OSHA compliance 'effectively insulat[es]' Harmon from tort liability; Privette exceptions may still matter.

citation_verdicts.existence_reasoning: California Code of Civil Procedure section 335.1 is a well-known California statute setting a two-year limitations period for assault, battery, injury to, or death of an individual caused by wrongful act or neglect.
citation_verdicts.known_actual_holding: Section 335.1 provides a two-year limitations period for personal injury and wrongful-death claims.
citation_verdicts.support_reasoning: The statute supports the asserted proposition that California personal-injury claims generally have a two-year statute of limitations. However, the brief's broader time-bar argument appears internally weak on the stated facts because a March 10, 2023 filing for a March 14, 2021 injury is within two years, absent some unusual accrual issue not apparent from the facts.

citation_verdicts.existence_reasoning: I do not recognize Torres v. Granite Falls Dev. Corp., 198 Cal.App.4th 223 (2011). The citation format is plausible, but I cannot verify the case or its subject matter from memory.
citation_verdicts.support_reasoning: Because I cannot verify the case or its holding, I cannot assess whether it supports any OSHA-compliance or safety/due-care proposition.
